question: Did the review adhere to pre-defined objectives and eligibility criteria?
Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers: Inclusion criteria were prospective clinical trials (phase II or beyond) comprising at least 10 cases with advanced biliary cancer only; anti-EGFR therapies were applied as the only targeted therapy with or without chemotherapy; and reported events or incidents of toxic effect (i.e., skin rash).

question: Were eligibility criteria unambiguous?
answers: Inclusion criteria were prospective clinical trials (phase II or beyond) comprising at least 10 cases with advanced biliary cancer only; anti-EGFR therapies were applied as the only targeted therapy with or without chemotherapy; and reported events or incidents of toxic effect (i.e., skin rash).

question: Were all restrictions in eligibility crieria based on study characteristics appropriate (e.g. date, sample size, study quality, outcomes measured)? If yes, indicate which study characteristic was an inclusion/exclusion criteria
answers: "Inclusion criteria were prospective clinical trials (phase II or beyond) comprising at least 10 cases"

question: Were any restrictions in eligibility criteria based on sources of info appropriate (e.g. publication status or format, language, availability of data)?
answers: The language was restricted to English.

question: Did the search include an appropriate range of databases/ electronic sources for published and unpublished reports?
answers: We carried out a systematic search of PubMed, Web of Science, Cochrane Library databases and abstracts from the American Society of Clinical Oncology annual meetings held between January 2013 and August 2017.

question: Were methods additional to database searching used to identify relevant reports?
answers: An additional search from relevant references was manually performed for further supplementation.

question: Were the terms and structure of the search strategy likely to retrieve as many eligible studies as possible?
Were search strategy restrictions based on date, publication format, or language appropriate?
answers:
The language was restricted to English.

We carried out a systematic search of PubMed, Web of Science, Cochrane Library databases and abstracts from the American Society of Clinical Oncology annual meetings held between January 2013 and August 2017.

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in selection of studies?
Were efforts made to minimise error in data collection?
Were sufficient study characteristics considered for both review authors and readers to be able to interpret the results?
Were all relevant study results collected for use in the synthesis?
Was risk of bias (or methodological quality) formally assessed using appropriate criteria?
Were efforts made to minimise error in risk of bias assessment?
Did the synthesis include all studies that it should?
answers: A total of 714 patients who received anti-EGFR treatment from 13 trials were available for analysis of all-grade skin rash, with an incidence ranging from 55.6-94.3%.

question: Were all pre-defined analyses reported or departures explained?
Was the synthesis appropriate given the nature and similarity in the research questions, study designs and outcomes across included studies?
answers:
Heterogeneity was evaluated by Cochrane's Q statistic. When the p value was < 0.1 the assumption of homogeneity was deemed invalid , and a random-effects model was used after meticulous exploration of causes of heterogeneity. Otherwise, when the p value was > 0.1, a fixed-effects model was preferred.

Using the random effects model, we found that all-grade skin rash occurred with an incidence of 78.2% (95% CI 70.4-84.3) in patients who received anti-EGFR treatment.

Fig. 2 Forest plot for metaanalysis of incidence of all-grade skin rash in patients with advanced biliary cancers who were treated with anti-EGFR therapies. The fixed-effects model was used for patients treated with cetuximab (a). The random-effects model was used for patients treated with erlotinib (b), panitumumab (c) or
overall anti-EGFR therapies (d) ...

question: Was between-study variation (heterogeneity) minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers:
The heterogeneity of the 13 trials included was detected (Q = 56.0, p < 0.001). Potential causes for heterogeneity were explored by subgroup analysis of a different type of anti-EGFR treatment.
We found that heterogeneity exists in erlotinib- or
panitumumab-based trials, although it was not detected in
cetuximab-based trials (Fig. 2) in which a relative fixed dose
was used with similar chemotherapy regimens. The observed
heterogeneity was possibly due to a dose difference and
different chemotherapy combinations

question: Were the findings robust, e.g. as demonstrated through funnel plot or sensitivity analyses?
Were biases in primary studies minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
Did the interpretation of findings address all of the concerns identified in Domains 1 to 4?
answers:
This study has several limitations. First, this is a metaanalysis
carried out at study level, meaning that different
confounding factors from the patient level are unable to be
assessed and incorporated into the analysis. Second, the
incidents of all-grade and high-grade skin rash demonstrated
significant heterogeneity among different studies.
This might be explained in part by the fact that differences
occur in treatment type, drug dose, concomitant chemotherapy,
study population, and CTCAE versions for recording
adverse events. Third, some studies did not report a correlation
between rash and clinical benefit, which might lead to
reduced power of our stratified analysis to reach a definitive
conclusion. However, the current results were still significant
and encouraging. Fourth, two studies [8, 31] recorded
the side-effects as skin toxicity instead of rash, which might
overestimate the incidence of anti-EGFR-induced rash,
although rash is a classical and indispensable stage of anti-
EGFR-induced toxic effects on the skin . Finally, the
relatively low incidence of biliary cancer and corresponding
small sample sizes involved in the current analysis
might limit our findings, although pooled data were already
endowed with much more statistical power than individual
trials.

question: Was the relevance of identified studies to the review's research question appropriately considered?
answers:
We yielded 276 articles after the initial literature search, from which 17 potentially relevant clinical trials [5-9, 24-32, 37-39] were further selected for assessment (Fig. 1). Three trials were excluded because the number of
patients was <10 (n = 1) ; inclusion of other targeted
therapies (n = 1) ; and inclusion of the same patient
cohort from published data (n = 1) .

question: Did the reviewers avoid emphasizing results on the basis of their statistical significance?

Did the research questions and inclusion criteria for the review include the components of PICO?
answers: Inclusion criteria were prospective clinical trials (phase II or beyond) comprising at least 10 cases with advanced biliary cancer only; anti-EGFR therapies were applied as the only targeted therapy with or without chemotherapy; and reported events or incidents of toxic effect (i.e., skin rash).

question: If they performed quantitative synthesis did the review authors carry out an adequate investigation of publication bias (small study bias) and discuss its likely impact on the results of the review?
